steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers caution against concluding something. The key detail is that supplementation data was "rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked" — so any conclusion drawn from that data is unreliable.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'vitamin D levels among zoo chimpanzees likely show different seasonal patterns than wild chimpanzees': This is about seasonal patterns, not supplementation. The caution is specifically about what the flawed supplementation data can prove. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the lack of effect may reflect data shortcomings rather than actual ineffectiveness': This is actually what the researchers ARE saying — they acknowledge the data is flawed and that the null result may not be real. This is their position, not what they caution against. ✗
  • Choice C — 'supplemental vitamin D has no promise as a strategy for offsetting winter reductions': This is a definitive negative conclusion drawn directly from the flawed supplementation data. The researchers caution against exactly this: declaring supplementation worthless based on unreliable data. ✓
  • Choice D — 'winter reductions in UVB can contribute to vitamin D level variations': This conclusion comes from the seasonal UVB data, which was well-documented — not from the supplementation data that was poorly tracked. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
